Q1: My study routine was honestly quite scattered because I was juggling so many things at once. I tried to dedicate about 24 hours a week to studying, but it rarely happened in neat blocks. I'd often study late at night after my extracurricular commitments, or squeeze in reading during breaks. I relied heavily on writing things down and making my own notes from the course materials, which worked better for me than just watching videos.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying a bit each day, but then deadlines would pile up and I'd have to do marathon sessions. My attendance wasn't great because I sometimes had to prioritize other responsibilities, but I always tried to catch up with the materials on my own time. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work.
Q3: Usually a deadline would prompt me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd also log in when I needed to check discussion posts or see if there were any announcements about assignments. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because they felt less overwhelming than regular assignments. But I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse—I usually had a specific purpose in mind.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, especially when the gamified elements were active and I could see my progress. It felt like a small accomplishment to complete those tasks, which helped during weeks when my anxiety was particularly high. But I'll admit the routine never fully stuck because life kept interrupting. When things got chaotic, the LMS was one of the first things to slip.
Q5: The written materials and reading resources were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs, highlight them, and make my own notes in my notebook. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could see how other students were interpreting the material, which sometimes clarified things I was confused about. The gamified tasks gave me small wins that helped with my anxiety around bigger assignments.
Q6: I honestly didn't watch many of the videos—maybe just over ten minutes total. They felt time-consuming and I couldn't easily take notes from them the way I could with written content. I'd often tell myself I'd go back and watch them later, but later never came. Some optional readings also got pushed aside when I was overwhelmed with required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to the assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand something I needed for assessment, I was motivated to engage with it. The gamified tasks felt worthwhile because they gave immediate feedback and a sense of progress, which helped manage my anxiety about whether I was doing enough.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd skip when I was stressed or short on time. Required activities had a deadline attached, so there was external pressure to complete them. With optional ones, I always thought I'd get to them eventually, but the urgency wasn't there. My anxiety actually made me focus almost exclusively on what was mandatory because I was terrified of failing.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and work from printed copies or my tablet without being connected. I also used my own handwritten notes extensively, rewriting key concepts to help them stick. Sometimes I'd look up additional explanations on other websites or YouTube if the course material wasn't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from my printed notes or downloaded materials, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd spend hours rewriting summaries, testing myself with flashcards I made, or discussing concepts with classmates in person. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in, but I was actually working hard during those times.
Q11: Yes, this was my main approach actually. I'd download everything I needed in one session, then study from those files for days without logging back in. It was more efficient for me because I could study without internet distractions. My LMS activity probably looked minimal during those stretches, but I was genuinely engaged with the content offline.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, usually when my anxiety spiked or when I had multiple deadlines across different courses. Sometimes I'd feel paralyzed by how much there was to do and end up doing nothing for a day or two. My attendance dropped during those times because just showing up felt overwhelming. But I always managed to pull myself together eventually—I've learned to push through difficult moments.
Q13: Competing responsibilities affected me significantly. Between extracurriculars and personal obligations, I often had to make tough choices about where to spend my time. When I was fatigued, the LMS was harder to engage with because it required active effort. Confusion about certain topics also slowed me down—I'd avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them for an assignment.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without really processing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd scroll through pages or have a video playing in the background while thinking about something completely different. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they might have looked like engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, especially when they felt too big or when I wasn't sure how to approach them. My anxiety sometimes made starting feel impossible, so I'd do easier tasks first to feel productive. I knew the important things were waiting, but facing them required mental energy I didn't always have. I'd usually complete them eventually, just not as early as I should have.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for using the LMS. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, downloading materials and checking requirements. Without a deadline, I'd often let things slide, telling myself I had time. The pressure of a due date cut through my avoidance and forced me to engage, even when my anxiety was high.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes not in the way instructors might hope. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going and reduced my anxiety about my performance. But if feedback was critical or I did worse than expected, I'd sometimes avoid logging in for a while because I didn't want to face more of the same. Eventually I'd use critical feedback to improve, but I needed time to process it first.
Q18: The course structure with gamified elements actually helped me because it broke things into smaller, manageable pieces. When everything felt overwhelming, those smaller tasks gave me entry points. However, the overall structure still felt demanding with twelve online courses to manage. I appreciated having flexibility in when I could engage, but sometimes that flexibility let me procrastinate too much.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt less intimidating than big assignments. Having clear milestones helped me see progress, which was important for managing my anxiety. What made engagement harder was when materials were only available as long videos or when instructions weren't clear. I needed things to be straightforward because I was already juggling so much mentally.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things without actually being able to focus and produce work. They might be logging in repeatedly, looking at materials, trying to understand what's expected, but feeling too overwhelmed to actually complete submissions. It's like being stuck in preparation mode without being able to move forward. I've definitely experienced moments like that myself.
Q21: That actually describes my situation somewhat. My LMS activity was relatively low, but I still managed to earn a Merit grade. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. The exam and assignments reflected studying that never showed up in the system. I think students who know how they learn best can be efficient without high visible activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are most active or which resources get accessed frequently. They can track completion of specific tasks and time spent on the platform. But they miss everything that happens offline—the note-taking, the thinking, the discussions with peers outside the system. They also can't capture the quality of engagement or whether someone was actually focused while logged in.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, making connections, and caring about my learning. For me, engagement often looks like rewriting notes, asking myself questions, and discussing ideas with others. It's more about mental investment than time spent on any platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety while still pushing through to complete the course. It wouldn't capture the resilience it took to keep going when I felt overwhelmed or the offline hours I spent studying from printed materials. It also wouldn't show the peer support I received and gave outside the platform, or how the gamified elements helped me manage my mental state. My engagement was real, just not always visible.
